rescue_reasoning: Keep as climate-relevant mitigation. The policy explicitly establishes an energy benchmarking and disclosure requirement: it "requires owners of multifamily and commercial properties larger than 10,000 sq. ft. to annually report whole-building energy and water consumption data to NYSERDA for public disclosure" and directs utilities to "provide automatic aggregated whole-building uploads... to... ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager." Benchmarking/disclosure is a standard mitigation mechanism that drives energy efficiency actions via transparency and market/managerial signals even without an explicit GHG/emissions keyword. climate_screen is `explicit_self`, so it should be retained despite the missing mitigation outcome token.
policy_statement: By 2024, commence a statewide energy benchmarking and disclosure program requiring owners of multifamily and commercial properties >10,000 sq ft to annually report whole-building energy and water data to NYSERDA for public disclosure, and require utilities to provide automatic aggregated whole-building uploads to ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager.
mechanism_description: The program requires multifamily and commercial building owners to annually report whole-building energy and water data for public disclosure (with utilities providing aggregated uploads), creating transparency that can drive efficiency improvements.
primary_causal_pathway: Information transparency/market signal → efficiency actions (O&M + retrofits) → ↓ building energy use → ↓ fossil energy combustion → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Cities require or facilitate energy/water benchmarking, monitoring, and/or public reporting so owners, tenants, lenders, and regulators can compare performance and identify inefficiencies.  
(2) Mediating physical/process link: Improved data availability and comparability triggers operational improvements (commissioning, controls tuning), capital upgrades (HVAC, envelope, lighting), and behavioral changes; these measures reduce kWh and on-site fuel consumption for the same building service levels.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower energy demand reduces upstream grid emissions and direct combustion emissions, decreasing overall GHG emissions from the buildings sector.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building energy consumption leading to lower GHG emissions from electricity generation and on-site fuel use.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: transparency and benchmarking → motivates/identifies efficiency improvements → lowers energy use → lowers GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while water data and energy-burden reporting can support equity and resource efficiency, the core mechanism is to drive building energy efficiency, with the main climate impact being emissions reduction (mitigation).  
3) Edge cases applied: if disclosure is used purely for equity planning or billing assistance without driving efficiency actions, climate impact could be indirect; however, the canonical mechanism specified is explicitly “market signal for efficiency,” keeping mitigation dominant.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy that expands benchmarking/monitoring/disclosure to create performance comparisons and motivate efficiency improvements shares the same primary mitigation pathway through reduced building energy use.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: whether outcomes come mainly from operational changes vs retrofits, and whether electricity vs gas dominates, must be determined at the individual policy level with implementation details and local energy mix.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some related policies emphasize water benchmarking or “energy burden” reporting; those can be primarily equity/resource-planning tools unless explicitly tied to efficiency interventions. Municipal-only monitoring may yield smaller market signaling effects but still targets efficiency-driven emissions reductions.
key_indicators: “energy benchmarking and disclosure program”; “requires owners… to annually report whole-building energy and water consumption data”; “to NYSERDA for public disclosure”; “PSC should require… utilities to provide automatic aggregated whole-building uploads”; “ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager”
co_benefits: Improved water management/conservation (via water consumption reporting); potential consumer/tenant information benefits and market transparency beyond emissions impacts.
instance_edge_case_notes: Although utilities are “required” to provide automated uploads (a regulatory mandate element), the overall policy mechanism is best classified as information-disclosure because the core instrument is mandatory reporting/public disclosure intended to induce efficiency actions via transparency rather than prescribing specific energy performance outcomes.
